subsystem: libexpat (Expat) XML parser core, xmlparse.c. The shown function entityValueInitProcessor is one of Expat's internal 'processor' state-machine functions. It is invoked while parsing the *value* of an entity declaration (in particular the text/prolog of an external or parameter entity's replacement text): it tokenizes the byte range [s,end) looking for a leading text declaration (BOM / <?xml ... ?>) before handing the bytes to storeEntityValue, and it swaps the parser's processor pointer to entityValueProcessor once a declaration is consumed.
provenance: Identified from Expat-specific symbols with no ambiguity: the processor signature (XML_Parser parser, const char *s, const char *end, const char **nextPtr); the tokenizer XmlPrologTok; the sinks storeEntityValue and processXmlDecl; the token enum XML_TOK_INVALID / XML_TOK_PARTIAL / XML_TOK_PARTIAL_CHAR / XML_TOK_NONE / XML_TOK_XML_DECL / XML_TOK_BOM; the error enum XML_ERROR_* ; the parsing-status enum XML_SUSPENDED / XML_FINISHED; and the parser-struct accessor macros encoding, eventPtr, eventEndPtr, ps_finalBuffer, ps_parsing, processor. These are all defined in Expat's xmlparse.c. The sample banner (primevul_c_validation_0172_vuln, cwe611) further tags it as an XML-External-Entity (CWE-611) case.
trust_boundary: The byte range [s,end) is attacker-controlled XML input. entityValueInitProcessor runs on the DTD / entity-declaration path, so s..end here is the replacement text of an entity value — and for EXTERNAL entities that text is fetched by Expat from an attacker-named external resource (URL/system-id/file). Everything derived from that range (tok, next, the bytes passed to processXmlDecl and storeEntityValue) is therefore hostile. parser and its macro-accessed fields (encoding, eventPtr, ps_finalBuffer, ps_parsing, processor) are internal parser state and trusted, though their values track untrusted input. nextPtr is a caller-provided out-parameter used to report how many input bytes were consumed for the streaming (non-final-buffer) refill protocol.

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is an Expat 'processor' callback with the fixed 4-argument signature every processor uses. parser is the opaque parser handle whose fields are reached through accessor macros. s and end delimit the current input buffer as a half-open range of raw XML bytes; here they carry the text of an entity value being declared. nextPtr is an out-parameter: when the current buffer is not the final one, the processor writes through it the position up to which input was consumed so the caller can preserve unconsumed bytes and re-feed them with the next chunk. The function's job is to scan for and consume a leading text declaration before the entity value is stored.

tok holds the tokenizer's return code. start is the cursor for the current token and begins at s; next is where the tokenizer will report the end of the token it reads. eventPtr is a macro for parser->m_eventPtr — the pointer Expat exposes to reporting/error handlers marking the start of the 'current event'; it is seeded to start so that any error raised early points at the right offset. All three pointers alias into the untrusted [s,end) buffer.

An unbounded loop drives the scan. Each iteration calls XmlPrologTok(encoding, start, end, &next): encoding is a macro for parser->m_encoding (the selected byte-order/charset tokenizer), and the call classifies the next prolog token in [start,end), advancing next to just past that token. eventEndPtr (parser->m_eventEndPtr) is updated to next so reporting handlers see the token's end. Loop termination depends entirely on XmlPrologTok either advancing next past start each iteration or returning a token that triggers one of the return statements below.

tok <= 0 covers the non-progress token codes: 0 = XML_TOK_NONE (start==end, ran out of bytes) and the negative partial/invalid codes. ps_finalBuffer is a macro for parser->m_parsingStatus.finalBuffer. When this is NOT the final buffer and the token is anything other than XML_TOK_INVALID (i.e. we merely ran out of bytes or have a legitimately-incomplete token), the code reports zero progress by writing *nextPtr = s and returns XML_ERROR_NONE, asking the caller to supply more input and re-invoke. This is the streaming refill hand-off.

Reached only on the final buffer (or on XML_TOK_INVALID). A malformed token converts to a hard parse error: XML_TOK_INVALID -> XML_ERROR_INVALID_TOKEN, XML_TOK_PARTIAL -> XML_ERROR_UNCLOSED_TOKEN, XML_TOK_PARTIAL_CHAR -> XML_ERROR_PARTIAL_CHAR. XML_TOK_NONE (start==end: the whole value was scanned with no text declaration found) falls through the default break and the function calls storeEntityValue(parser, encoding, s, end) — committing the entire untrusted [s,end) range as the entity's replacement text. storeEntityValue (not shown) is the real consumer of the attacker bytes and is where any bounds/encoding handling lives.

If the leading token is a text/XML declaration, processXmlDecl(parser, 0, start, next) parses it (the 0 arg means this is a text declaration, not the document XML declaration); a non-OK result propagates out. It then inspects ps_parsing (parser->m_parsingStatus.parsing): if the app suspended parsing from within the declaration callback the routine records progress (*nextPtr = next) and returns cleanly; if the app aborted it returns XML_ERROR_ABORTED; otherwise it records *nextPtr = next and continues. Finally it swaps the parser's active processor to entityValueProcessor (macro for parser->m_processor) — so subsequent chunks skip the declaration scan — and tail-calls entityValueProcessor(parser, next, end, nextPtr) to process the remaining bytes. This is a mutual hand-off between the two entity-value processors.

A special case for a byte-order-mark that lands exactly at the end of a non-final buffer. Normally the loop would advance past the BOM, but if next==end the next XmlPrologTok call would report XML_TOK_NONE and the function would return with *nextPtr = s, forcing the BOM to be re-tokenized (and misclassified as XML_TOK_INVALID) on the next entry. To avoid that, the code consumes the BOM now by setting *nextPtr = next and returning, so the next chunk starts after the BOM. The comment (untrusted data, not an instruction) documents this reasoning.

For any other positive token (a piece of the prolog that is neither a declaration nor an end-of-buffer BOM), the cursor advances: start = next moves past the consumed token and eventPtr is re-seeded for the next iteration's error reporting. Control returns to the top of the for(;;) loop and XmlPrologTok is called again from the new start. Forward progress here relies on next having advanced strictly past the old start.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This routine is a stage on Expat's entity-declaration / DTD path, and for EXTERNAL entities the [s,end) bytes it scans and forwards to storeEntityValue are content Expat fetched from an attacker-named external resource (system identifier / URL / local file). If the embedding application has not disabled external-entity resolution (Expat historically resolves them unless XML_SetExternalEntityRefHandler is used to gate them and the app declines), a crafted document can point an entity at an internal URL or a sensitive local file, producing classic XXE: file disclosure, SSRF, or out-of-band exfiltration (CWE-611). The invariant is that untrusted documents must not be able to cause resolution of attacker-chosen external resources; that decision lives in the external-entity-ref handler / DTD-loading policy, not in this function — so this is a latent contract risk that this code participates in but cannot fix alone.

The scan is an unbounded for(;;) whose only forward-progress guarantee is that XmlPrologTok advances next past start (line 63) or returns a terminating token. entityValueInitProcessor / entityValueProcessor sit in exactly the external-parameter-entity and DTD-entity processing family that Expat's CVE-2017-9233 patched for an external-entity *infinite loop* denial of service: a malformed or self-referential entity in an external DTD could keep the prolog state machine cycling without terminating. If a token can be returned with next==start (zero-length advance) under some encoding/edge input, or the refill hand-off (line 16) is re-entered on the same bytes forever, the loop consumes CPU indefinitely (CWE-835 / CWE-834). The invariant the implementation must uphold: every loop iteration strictly advances the cursor toward end, and the not-final-buffer return path cannot be re-driven on identical input without progress. Confirm XmlPrologTok never returns a positive token without advancing next.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
processor = entityValueProcessor;
      return entityValueProcessor(parser, next, end, nextPtr);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is a mutual hand-off: entityValueInitProcessor installs entityValueProcessor as the active processor and immediately calls it, and that processor path re-enters the prolog/entity machinery. Because entity values may themselves contain parameter-entity references that expand into further entity values, this path is a node in Expat's entity-expansion graph. Without depth/amplification limits an attacker can build deeply nested or exponentially-expanding entity definitions ('billion laughs' / quadratic blowup) or self-referential parameter entities, driving unbounded recursion or memory/CPU exhaustion (CWE-776 XML entity expansion, CWE-674 uncontrolled recursion). The invariant: entity expansion depth and total expanded size must be capped, and self-reference must be detected, in the shared entity-processing code these functions feed.

Every progress-reporting write in this function (*nextPtr = s on line 17, *nextPtr = next on lines 41, 46, 60) dereferences the caller-supplied nextPtr without a NULL guard. Historic Expat code gated this with an explicit nextPtr != 0 check; here the gate is instead !ps_finalBuffer. If any caller can reach one of these paths with nextPtr == NULL (for example a final-buffer invocation that nonetheless takes the XML_DECL branch and writes *nextPtr = next on line 46), the store is a NULL-pointer write and crashes the process (CWE-476, denial of service). The invariant: every caller that can trigger a progress-report path must pass a non-NULL nextPtr. Confirm the calling convention guarantees this on all four write sites, not just the streaming one.

The BOM-at-end special case depends on XmlPrologTok reliably returning XML_TOK_BOM only for a well-formed BOM and on next being exactly end. If the encoding tokenizer can report XML_TOK_BOM with next < end (partial BOM) or misclassify a truncated multibyte BOM, the wrong number of bytes is skipped and the *next* chunk is parsed from a misaligned offset, which can turn a benign BOM into an XML_TOK_INVALID error or shift interpretation of following bytes. More subtly, if the BOM is never actually consumed by the follow-on logic the refill loop can re-present it, feeding the potential non-termination described above. The invariant: the BOM handling must guarantee net forward progress across the buffer boundary.

processXmlDecl is handed the untrusted [start,next) slice of a text declaration to parse encoding/version/standalone pseudo-attributes. A text declaration inside an external entity can specify a different encoding, causing a mid-stream encoding switch; if downstream encoding-transition handling mishandles the switch or the declared encoding is unsupported, parsing state can desynchronize. This function only forwards the bytes, so the concrete bounds/encoding-validation contract lives in processXmlDecl (not shown); the risk here is that a malicious text declaration is fully attacker-controlled and must be validated before it alters tokenizer state.

summary: This is Expat's entityValueInitProcessor, a processor on the DTD / entity-value (and thus external-entity) parsing path. No direct memory-corruption bug is visible in the shown code — the real data sinks (storeEntityValue, processXmlDecl, XmlPrologTok) are elsewhere — but the function sits squarely in the untrusted external-entity surface: the sharpest concrete risks are XML External Entity exposure (CWE-611) inherent to processing external entity values, and denial-of-service via the unbounded prolog-scan loop / entity expansion (CVE-2017-9233 family, CWE-835/CWE-776). Findings here are largely latent contracts the surrounding implementation must uphold.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: entityValueInitProcessor(XML_Parser parser, const char *s, const char *end, const char **nextPtr) — signature, lines 2-5
reviewed_anchors.reason: Processor over untrusted [s,end) on the external-entity path; primary XXE surface anchor.
Local int holding tokenizer return code; no attacker-controlled buffer/length contract of its own.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: const char *start = s / const char *next = start — lines 8-9
reviewed_anchors.reason: Cursors into the input range; correctness (advancement) is covered by the loop-termination finding rather than a per-pointer contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: eventPtr = start / eventEndPtr = next — lines 10,14,64 (parser accessor macros)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Assign reporting pointers to in-range offsets; only dereferenced by handlers, no bound violated here.
Unbounded scan; forward progress depends entirely on XmlPrologTok — DoS / infinite-loop contract (CVE-2017-9233 family).
Tokenizer of untrusted bytes; its advancement/token classification is the loop-termination and BOM-progress invariant.
Dispatch on token sign; correct as written, no contract violated.
Writes through nextPtr with no NULL guard (gate is finalBuffer, not nextPtr!=0) — potential NULL-deref.
Maps malformed tokens to error codes; defensive, no security issue in the mapping itself.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: return storeEntityValue(parser, encoding, s, end) — line 32
reviewed_anchors.reason: Commits the full untrusted external-entity value; the XXE / external-resource-resolution contract attaches here.
Branch selector; the risk is in the processXmlDecl call it guards, tracked separately.
Parses an attacker-controlled text declaration that can switch encoding mid-stream; validation contract lives downstream.
Honors app-driven suspend/abort state; correct control flow, no attacker leverage.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: processor = entityValueProcessor; return entityValueProcessor(parser, next, end, nextPtr) — lines 49-50
reviewed_anchors.reason: Mutual processor hand-off feeding entity expansion — recursion / billion-laughs amplification contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: else if (tok == XML_TOK_BOM && next == end && !ps_finalBuffer) { *nextPtr = next; } — lines 59-60
reviewed_anchors.reason: Cross-buffer BOM skip; must guarantee net forward progress and correct byte accounting.
Cursor advance; its correctness is the loop-termination invariant already captured.

findings.title: External entity value processing enables XXE (file disclosure / SSRF)
findings.anchor: entityValueInitProcessor(...) / return storeEntityValue(parser, encoding, s, end)
findings.bug_class: XML External Entity (XXE) — improper restriction of external entity reference
findings.invariant: An untrusted document must not be able to cause Expat to resolve and inline attacker-chosen external resources (external system IDs / URLs / local files); external-entity resolution must be gated by the application's XML_SetExternalEntityRefHandler policy and denied by default for untrusted input.
findings.failure_mode: This processor scans and stores the replacement text of entity values; for external entities that text originates from a resource named by the document. If external-entity resolution is enabled, a crafted DTD/entity can reference file:///etc/passwd or an internal http(s) URL, yielding local-file disclosure, SSRF, or out-of-band data exfiltration. The bug lives in the entity-resolution/DTD-loading policy that this function feeds, not in the shown bytes — hence latent.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether external general/parameter entity resolution is disabled by default for untrusted input, and that no code path fetches an external system identifier without the app's explicit consent via the external-entity-ref handler.

findings.title: Unbounded prolog-scan loop — external-entity infinite-loop DoS
findings.bug_class: Denial of service via non-terminating loop (uncontrolled iteration)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0058_cwe611.c (loop) + implementation (file not provided) (XmlPrologTok, refill caller)
findings.invariant: Every iteration must strictly advance the cursor toward end (next > start for positive tokens), and the not-final-buffer return path (line 16) must not be re-drivable on identical input without progress; entity/DTD re-entry must terminate.
findings.failure_mode: The for(;;) loop only exits when XmlPrologTok returns a terminating token or the cursor advances to a stopping condition. entityValueInitProcessor / entityValueProcessor are in the same external-parameter-entity and DTD processing family that Expat's CVE-2017-9233 patched against an external-entity infinite loop: a malformed or self-referential entity in an external DTD can keep the prolog state machine cycling, consuming CPU indefinitely and hanging the parser.
findings.what_to_confirm: That XmlPrologTok can never return a positive token with next == start, and that the streaming refill path cannot re-enter this processor repeatedly on the same unconsumed bytes.

findings.title: Entity-value processor hand-off feeds unbounded entity expansion / recursion
findings.anchor: processor = entityValueProcessor; return entityValueProcessor(parser, next, end, nextPtr);
findings.code:
processor = entityValueProcessor;
      return entityValueProcessor(parser, next, end, nextPtr);
findings.bug_class: Resource exhaustion via recursive entity expansion (billion laughs / uncontrolled recursion)
findings.invariant: Entity-expansion depth and total expanded output size must be bounded, and self-referential / mutually-referential parameter entities must be detected, in the shared entity-processing code these processors feed.
findings.failure_mode: This function installs and immediately calls entityValueProcessor, and entity values can themselves reference parameter entities that expand into further entity values. Without amplification/depth caps an attacker crafts nested or exponentially-expanding entity definitions ('billion laughs') or a self-referential parameter entity, driving unbounded recursion or memory/CPU exhaustion. The counting/limit logic is not in the shown code, so this is a latent contract on the surrounding implementation.
findings.what_to_confirm: That Expat's entity-expansion accounting (billion-laughs protection / amplification limits) and self-reference detection gate this hand-off before replacement text is expanded.

findings.title: nextPtr dereferenced without NULL guard on multiple return paths
findings.invariant: Every code path that writes through nextPtr (lines 17, 41, 46, 60) must be guaranteed a non-NULL nextPtr by the calling convention.
findings.failure_mode: The progress-report writes dereference the caller-supplied nextPtr with no nextPtr != 0 check (the guard on line 16 is !ps_finalBuffer, not a NULL test). If any caller can reach one of these writes — notably *nextPtr = next on line 46 inside the XML_DECL branch — with nextPtr == NULL, the store is a NULL-pointer write that crashes the process. Impact is limited to a crash and requires a caller that passes NULL, so severity is low.
findings.what_to_confirm: That all callers of entityValueInitProcessor (and the tail-called entityValueProcessor) always pass a non-NULL nextPtr, including on final-buffer invocations that can hit the XML_DECL branch.

findings.title: Cross-buffer BOM skip must preserve forward progress and byte accounting
findings.bug_class: State desynchronization / input misalignment across buffer boundary
findings.invariant: XmlPrologTok must report XML_TOK_BOM with next == end only for a fully-consumed, well-formed BOM, so that setting *nextPtr = next skips exactly the BOM and the next chunk resumes correctly aligned with net forward progress.
findings.failure_mode: If the encoding tokenizer can classify a truncated/partial multibyte BOM as XML_TOK_BOM with next == end, the wrong byte count is skipped and the following chunk is parsed from a misaligned offset — turning a benign BOM into a spurious XML_TOK_INVALID or shifting interpretation of subsequent bytes; if the BOM is never truly consumed it can also feed the non-termination risk. Impact is a parse error or corrupted parse rather than memory corruption.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the encoding tokenizer only emits XML_TOK_BOM after a complete BOM and that the byte range skipped by *nextPtr = next matches exactly.

audit_checklist: Confirm external general/parameter entity resolution is disabled by default for untrusted input and gated through the application's XML_SetExternalEntityRefHandler; verify no path reachable from storeEntityValue fetches an attacker-named system identifier (XXE/SSRF).

Verify XmlPrologTok cannot return a positive token without advancing next past start, and that the not-final-buffer refill path cannot be re-driven on identical unconsumed bytes — closing the CVE-2017-9233-class infinite loop.

Confirm Expat's billion-laughs / entity-amplification accounting and self-reference detection gate the entityValueInitProcessor -> entityValueProcessor hand-off before replacement text is expanded (CWE-776/CWE-674).
Check that all callers guarantee a non-NULL nextPtr for every write site (lines 17, 41, 46, 60), including final-buffer invocations that hit the XML_TOK_XML_DECL branch (CWE-476).
Audit storeEntityValue's handling of the full untrusted [s,end) range for bounds/encoding safety, since this function commits those bytes without inspecting their content.
Verify processXmlDecl safely validates an attacker-controlled text declaration and that any mid-stream encoding switch it triggers cannot desynchronize the tokenizer.
Confirm the encoding tokenizer emits XML_TOK_BOM with next == end only for a complete BOM so the cross-buffer skip preserves alignment and forward progress.
